title: Repairing a stair balustrade spindle
subtitle: Remove a broken spindle, fit a replacement, and secure it in the handrail groove
excerpt: How to remove a cracked or loose balustrade spindle, source a matching replacement, cut it to the correct length and rake angle, and fix it back into the handrail and string using the original groove-and-pin or mortise system.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: should be repaired promptly; UK Building Regulations require no more than 100 mm (4 in) gap between spindles and the handrail must be able to withstand a lateral load. The replacement spindle must match the profile of the existing ones. Take a sample to a timber merchant or scan the profile for a match.
Most modern spindles sit in a routed groove in the underside of the handrail and are pinned into a similar groove in the top of the

. Older staircases may have mortised spindles. To remove a groove-and-pin spindle: tap the bottom sideways to slide it out of the groove, then angle and lift to free the top. If pinned, cut the pin flush and prise out with a chisel. If mortised, saw the spindle off flush and chop out the stub from the mortise.
Step 2, clean the groove or mortise

Remove old glue and debris from the groove or mortise with a chisel pared clean. The groove must be clear of any lumps that would prevent the replacement spindle from seating flat. Measure the depth and width of the groove at both ends to confirm the replacement spindle's foot and head dimensions will fit; older staircases sometimes have grooves of non-standard width.
Step 3, cut the replacement to length and rake

Measure the length of the adjacent spindle and transfer the length to the replacement. The top and bottom cuts are parallel but angled to the rake of the stair (typically 38 to 42 degrees). Set a sliding bevel or combination square to the rake angle from the string surface and use it to mark and cut both ends. Cut a little long and trim until the spindle fits snugly in both grooves without forcing.

Apply PVA glue to the foot of the spindle and press it into the string groove. Apply glue to the head and angle the spindle upward to engage the handrail groove. The spindle should be a push fit without needing a hammer: forcing a tight spindle can lift the handrail off its own fixings. Pin through the foot into the string groove with two oval nails to prevent rotation once the glue sets. Allow 2 to 4 hours before loading.

attrs.fix: Measure from the bottom of the string groove to the top of the handrail groove (the full inside distance), not from spindle face to face. Add the tongue length at each end to the body measurement.
attrs.cause: The length was measured from the spindle body but not accounting for the tongue that fits into each groove.
attrs.symptom: Replacement spindle is too short and does not reach both grooves

attrs.fix: Remove the spindle, clean the groove, and pack the sides with a thin timber shim before re-gluing and pinning. The shim eliminates the lateral play.
attrs.cause: The groove is wider than the spindle foot, or the glue joint failed.
attrs.symptom: Spindle rotates or rattles in the groove after fitting
